title: CC Exercises — Platform Foundation
description: Hands-on exercises covering the type system, Spring configuration, ServiceLayer, FlexibleSearch, interceptors, and extensions.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Extend the Type System
sections.content:
Objective

Create a custom item type with attributes, relations, and enums in items.xml, run a system update, and verify the type exists in the database and Backoffice.

Scenario

You are building a loyalty programme. You need a LoyaltyCard type that links to a Customer.

Steps

  1. In your custom extension's resources/*-items.xml, create:
    • An enumeration LoyaltyTier with values: BRONZE, SILVER, GOLD, PLATINUM.
    • An item type LoyaltyCard extending GenericItem with attributes: cardNumber (String, unique, mandatory), points (Integer, default 0), tier (LoyaltyTier enum), activeSince (Date).
    • A relation Customer2LoyaltyCard — one Customer has one optional LoyaltyCard (1:0..1).
  2. Run ant clean all and perform a system update (not initialize — preserve existing data).
  3. Verify in HAC → Console → FlexibleSearch: run SELECT {pk}, {cardNumber}, {tier} FROM {LoyaltyCard} — it should return an empty result set (no data yet, but the type exists).
  4. Open Backoffice and search for the LoyaltyCard type. Verify you can create a new instance manually.

Bonus Challenge

Add a deployment table setting to your type (deployment table="loyaltycards" typecode="30001"). After system update, verify the physical table name in the database matches your setting.

Validation

The type appears in FlexibleSearch, Backoffice, and the database. Creating a LoyaltyCard with all fields works. The relation to Customer is navigable from both sides.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Spring Bean Override & Custom Service
sections.content:
Objective

Create a custom service with a DAO, register it as a Spring bean, and override an existing platform bean using the alias mechanism.

Scenario

Build a LoyaltyService that manages LoyaltyCard operations, and override the default CustomerNameStrategy to append the loyalty tier to the customer's display name.

Steps

Part A — Custom Service & DAO

  1. Create a LoyaltyCardDao interface and DefaultLoyaltyCardDao implementation that provides: findByCardNumber(String cardNumber) and findByCustomer(CustomerModel customer). Use FlexibleSearch in the DAO.
  2. Create a LoyaltyService interface and DefaultLoyaltyService implementation with methods: getCard(CustomerModel customer), addPoints(String cardNumber, int points), calculateTier(int points) — returning BRONZE for 0-999, SILVER for 1000-4999, GOLD for 5000-9999, PLATINUM for 10000+.
  3. Register both beans in your extension's *-spring.xml. Inject the DAO into the service and ModelService for saving.

Part B — Bean Override

  1. Create a LoyaltyCustomerNameStrategy that extends the default DefaultCustomerNameStrategy.
  2. Override the getName() method: call super.getName(), then append " [GOLD]" (or the actual tier) if the customer has a LoyaltyCard.
  3. Register your strategy as an alias for the standard customerNameStrategy bean in your *-spring.xml.

Validation

Write a Groovy script in HAC that: creates a Customer, creates a LoyaltyCard with 5000 points, links them, and calls the name strategy. Verify the display name includes "[GOLD]". Change points to 500 and verify it shows "[BRONZE]".

sections.title: Exercise 3 — FlexibleSearch Queries
sections.content:
Objective

Write increasingly complex FlexibleSearch queries covering joins, subqueries, parameters, catalog version filtering, and performance considerations.

Steps

Use HAC → Console → FlexibleSearch for all queries. Test each query and verify the results.

  1. Basic query: Select all Products where the name contains "camera" (case-insensitive). Use the localised name attribute with a LIKE condition.
  2. Join query: Select all Products that belong to a specific Category. Join Product to Category via the CategoryProductRelation. Return product code, product name, and category name.
  3. Parameterised query: Write a query that finds all Orders for a specific customer (passed as a parameter ?customer) placed within the last 30 days. Use the creationtime attribute with a date comparison.
  4. Catalog-aware query: Select all Products from the Online version of the electronics product catalog. Explicitly join CatalogVersion and filter by version name = 'Online' and catalog ID = 'electronicsProductCatalog'.
  5. Subquery: Find all Customers who have placed more than 3 orders. Use a subquery with COUNT and GROUP BY HAVING.
  6. Performance query: Take query 2 and add ORDER BY {p.name} ASC and LIMIT 10 OFFSET 0 for pagination. Note: FlexibleSearch translates to SQL — understand that the database index on the name column affects performance.

Bonus Challenge

Disable the catalog version filter on a query using FlexibleSearchQuery.setDisableCatalogVersionFilter(true) in a Groovy script. Run the same product query and observe that results from both Staged and Online appear. Explain when this is useful (background jobs, data migration) and when it is dangerous (storefront queries returning unpublished products).

Validation

Each query returns the expected results. Document the SQL generated by FlexibleSearch (visible in the HAC console output) and compare it with your FlexibleSearch syntax to understand the translation.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — Build a Validate Interceptor
sections.content:
Objective

Create a ValidateInterceptor that enforces a business rule on your LoyaltyCard type, preventing invalid data from being saved.

Scenario

Business rule: a LoyaltyCard's points value must never be negative, and the cardNumber must follow the pattern LC-XXXXX (LC- followed by exactly 5 digits).

Steps

  1. Create a class LoyaltyCardValidateInterceptor implementing ValidateInterceptor<LoyaltyCardModel>.
  2. In the onValidate() method:
    • Check if points is negative → throw InterceptorException with message "Points cannot be negative".
    • Check if cardNumber does not match the regex LC-\d{5} → throw InterceptorException with message "Card number must follow pattern LC-XXXXX".
  3. Register the interceptor in *-spring.xml:
    <bean id="loyaltyCardValidateInterceptor"
  class="com.myext.interceptors.LoyaltyCardValidateInterceptor" />
<bean id="loyaltyCardValidateMapping"
  class="de.hybris.platform.servicelayer.interceptor.impl.InterceptorMapping">
  <property name="interceptor" ref="loyaltyCardValidateInterceptor" />
  <property name="typeCode" value="LoyaltyCard" />
</bean>
  4. Build and restart the server.

Validation

In HAC Groovy console: try to save a LoyaltyCard with points = -100 → verify the InterceptorException is thrown. Try cardNumber = "INVALID" → verify the exception. Try cardNumber = "LC-12345" with points = 100 → verify it saves successfully. Then test via Backoffice: try to create an invalid LoyaltyCard and verify the error message appears in the UI.
